All right. Hi, everyone. I'm Sam Carver and this is for the QA Specialist position at Novodia. So essentially jumping right in, first thing I like to do before editing a lesson is make sure I'm familiar with the rendering style, the style references, the background, core ideas, the age of the students, and the location of the students. I also like to get familiar with what the SME says about the lesson and things that I should be checking up on if there are any SME notes. Moving on from there, once I have all that information on Monk, I like to start at the top, go straight down, you know, top to bottom, left to right, default to tier 3, and I like to make sure that I keep my list of known bugs next to me, or known errors next to me, regarding like image generation, image matching, the image matching, the lesson, and the description, the audio matching, audio being aligned. Audio only saying what it's supposed to say rather than extra, making sure the age of the students on the screen, match the age of the students working through the screen, so high school or style schoolers, you know, not high schoolers working through elementary stuff on the screen, you know, the age should be similar, so making sure all of that stuff aligns, making sure that the activity type matches the questions so, and I'll get more into that later, but so yeah, I like to make sure that I keep my list next to me, my woods about me, and making sure I really see the small things, for example, like a head faced the correct way on a coin, you know, right or left, things like that. So yeah, my experience is pretty honed in on Novedia right now, I am the original unpaid intern, so, you know, an example of how I did catch an error and how I handled it would be when I was playing through the lesson as a student, and the question and the activity type didn't quite match, they weren't compatible, so the question was based on this image, what's the answer, and the image is behind all of the multiple choice answers and the multiple choice quiz, so the student couldn't see what the correct answer would be because it couldn't see that image, so, you know, it was a quick slack message over to Shawnee, and I said, you know, hey, I could put in a ticket for this, or should I change the activity type as there's something else I can do, knowing that I do have the capabilities within CG3 to do that, she got back to me and said, you know what, you can handle that by yourself, you should, you know, change the activity type to reading reply, and it's a perfect, and it was super awesome to take that agency, and, you know, make that change for myself and get that all smoothed out, all the variants and such, giving that student a smoother experience, you know, working through those issues on the screen to learn. So yeah, and the next question is kind of a perfect example of something that I could fix myself with kind of an intermediate, like a quick slack message, which is faster than a bug ticket, but, you know, things how to decide on if I can solve it by myself, or if I need help, usually falls back on me knowing what my capabilities are as, you know, the editing intern, what do I have the capability to do and what do I not, and sometimes, you know, I do ask, Shawnee, I reach out for help quickly to, you know, my teammates, my supervisors, things like that, and another way to do it is I like to do 30-10 split, so I'll work for 30 minutes, if I run into an issue, I'll work on that issue for 10 minutes, but I still can't get it solved, and if I don't want to send a message yet, I'll go back to working for 30 minutes, come back, spend another 10 minutes on it, and you know, if I still can't get it fixed after that 10 minutes, I'll work on something else for 30 minutes and I'll come back for another 10, and if at the end of that, you know, 10-10-10, so that whole entire 30-minute block, I will either, you know, compile some questions or have honed in my questions, you know, after trying all of these things, and I'll have written down everything I've tried to see if it worked or not on various issues, and so if nothing changes, I will submit a bug ticket or ask for help on Slack, but, you know, usually, usually, if I'm really wary, I'll end up waiting one day, like one overnight, because some of the issues do resolve themselves, like the generation issues, the loading issues, things like that. An example is the 404 issue I've seen with a lot of SMEs, can actually be solved with a re-upload of the activity, even though the upload button isn't highlighted as red, and the system doesn't indicate that you should, if you're getting those 404 messages, I've found through, you know, 30 minutes of trial and error, and control shift R, and things like that, that, you know, just a simple reload would help, so a lot of trial and error, and I max out at some point to actually get the issue solved, so, yeah, I'm Sam Carver, and I look forward to hearing from you guys.
